law: Realistically rendering reflections for multiple objects, or moving objects, may require multiple environment textures, each capturing the scene from a different point of view.
If your AR experience uses a rendering technology other than SceneKit, you’ll need to retrieve the generated textures yourself and determine how to use them appropriately in your shading engine.
In that method, use the property of each object to get the texture.

To more precisely define environment probes, choose environment texturing when you configure your AR session, then create your own instance for each virtual object you want to use environmental lighting with.
The and components of the probe’s extent should be three times the width and depth of the object, ensuring that the probe captures the area beneath and around the object.
Follow these tips to keep your app’s use of environment texturing realistic and efficient: Avoid virtual content that requires accurate reflections, such as mirror-finish surfaces.
ARKit environment textures don’t image the environment in all directions around the user, and don’t update in real time, so some kinds of content aren’t well suited for use in AR.
Design virtual content to use fully-reflective surfaces only in small or highly-detailed parts, and use less reflectivity in large flat surfaces.
Rendering a virtual object with realistic reflections require an environment probe that captures a small area around that object.
Don’t generate environment textures too often.
To run the sample, use an iOS device with an A12 chip or later.
By design, the user’s face anchor is always located behind the rear camera.
To serve the goal of mimicking the user with the robot head, the sample app applies the face anchor’s position to make the robot head always visible.
These anchors indicate each peer’s approximate location, which you can use, for example, to place virtual content that represents the peer user.
Although this sample draws its graphics using RealityKit, it doesn’t use RealityKit’s mechanism for over-the-network entity synchronization.
To aid ARKit with world map merging, a user must point their device near an area that another user has viewed.
Devices such as the second-generation iPad Pro 11-inch and fourth-generation iPad Pro 12.9-inch can use the LiDAR Scanner to calculate the distance of real-world objects from the user.
In order to avoid running an unsupported configuration, the sample app first checks whether the device supports scene depth.
Because the sample project draws to the screen using Metal, it converts the pixel buffer to a Metal texture as required to transfer the depth data to the GPU for rendering.
MPS requires input and output images that define the source and destination pixel data for the filter operation.
Since in-place MPS requires fallback code for devices that don’t support it, this sample project doesn’t use it.
Although this sample project doesn’t factor depth confidence into its fog effect, confidence data could filter out lower-accuracy depth values if the app’s algorithm required it.
Make sure the other device is in an area that the first device visited before sending the map, or has a similar view of the surrounding environment.
The other device displays a message indicating that it has received the map and is attempting to use it.
Requires Xcode 10.0, iOS 12.0 and two or more iOS devices with A9 or later processors.
For example, a multiplayer game design might require a host role to arbitrate gameplay.
The host uses to broadcast availability, and guests use to find a host to join.
Reliably sharing a map to another device requires two key steps: finding a good time to capture a map, and capturing and sending it.

To make use of it, the app uses to deserialize an object, then creates and runs a new using that map as the : ARKit then attempts to relocalize to the new world map—that is, to reconcile the received spatial-mapping information with what it senses of the local environment.

However, recording and transmitting a world map and relocalizing to a world map are time-consuming, bandwidth-intensive operations, so you should take those steps only once, when a new device joins a session.

To create an ongoing shared AR experience, where each user’s actions affect the AR scene visible to other users, after each device relocalizes to the same world map you should share only the information needed to recreate each user action.

For example, a game where users throw projectiles in the AR world space might define custom data types with attributes like initial position and velocity, then use Swift’s protocols to serialize that information to a binary representation for sending over the network.
This sample code supports and therefore, it requires ARKit 1.5 (iOS 11.3) or greater ARKit is not available in the iOS Simulator Building the sample requires Xcode 9.3 or later
Before you can use audio, you need to set up a session and place the object from which to play sound.

This sample loads the file from the bundle in : Then, the audio source is configured and prepared: When you’re ready to play the sound, create an , passing it the audio source: For best results, use mono audio files.
Use sound effects and environmental sound layers to create an engaging AR experience.
RealityKit requires iOS 13.
If a ray cast from the final screen location in the pan gesture doesn’t produce an intersection with a 3D world location, don’t move the sticky note there.
Keeping screen-space annotations to a minimum will maximize the user’s immersion in the AR experience.
But for similar reasons, you should enlarge a sticky note when the user is editing text.
Use the method to run a session with your configuration.

(This example app uses the method for the code shown below.) To use the detected image as a trigger for AR content, you’ll need to know its position and orientation, its size, and which reference image it is.
If your AR content depends on the extent of the image in the scene, you can then use the reference image’s to set up your content, as shown in the code below.
To use your own images for detection (in this sample or in your own project), you’ll need to add them to your asset catalog in Xcode.
Open your project’s asset catalog, then use the Add button (+) to add a new AR resource group.

For each image, use the inspector to describe the physical size of the image as you’d expect to find it in the user’s real-world environment, and optionally include a descriptive name for your own use.
Use separate resource groups to hold sets of images for use in separate sessions.
For example, an art museum app might use separate sessions (and thus separate resource groups) for detecting paintings in different wings of the museum.
Use only images on flat surfaces for detection.
Follow the tips below to design AR experiences that use image detection well.
Devices require the LiDAR Scanner to access the scene’s depth.
The sample project positions each point in GPU code, so the CPU side packages the depth data in a Metal texture for use on the GPU.
The sample project colors each point in GPU code, so the CPU side packages the camera data for use on the GPU.
The GPU color format is RGBA, which requires the sample project to convert the camera data to display it.
To see an example that extracts static conversion factors to a 4 x 4 matrix, see .
The point cloud view packages up several textures that its corresponding GPU code requires as input.
Similarly, the point cloud view packages up several calculated properties that its corresponding GPU code requires as input.
The AR provider converts the input depth data to RGB format, as required by the guided filter.
The shader (see ) converts the depth values into RGB, as required to display them.

This sample renders its graphics using RealityKit, but you can follow the same steps to use people occlusion with SceneKit.
However, if the user’s device doesn’t support people occlusion, you should continue your AR experience without it.
Alternatively, the frame semantic gives you the option of always occluding virtual content with any people that ARKit perceives in the camera feed irrespective of depth.
ARKit requires a device with an A9 or later processor.
To enable your app to detect real-world surfaces, you use a world tracking configuration.
For ARKit to establish tracking, the user must physically move their device to allow ARKit to get a sense of perspective.

To figure out where to put the square in the real world, you use an to ask ARKit where any surfaces exist in the real world.
Because the object’s position is transitory, use a instead of using a tracked ray cast.
In this case, a one-time hit test is appropriate because you don’t need refined position results over time for these requests.
While dragging, you avoid quick changes in orientation by subtracting the gesture’s rotation from the current object rotation.

You can use saved anchors to restore virtual content from a previous session.

For example: An app for visualizing furniture from a fixed catalog might save an identifier for each placed object in the corresponding anchor’s , then use that identifier to determine which 3D model to display when resuming a session from a saved map.
One way to build compelling AR experiences is to recognize features of the user’s environment and use them to trigger the appearance of virtual content.

This sample code project provides multiple ways to make use of object detection: Run the app to scan a real-world object and export a reference object file, which you can use in your own apps to detect that object.
Use the and classes as demonstrated in this sample app to record reference objects as part of your own asset production pipeline.
Use in a world-tracking AR session to recognize a reference object and create AR interactions.
ARKit requires an iOS device with A9 processor or later.
Use these recommendations as a target configuration even if it’s unreachable in the specific circumstances of your scanning environment.
Light the object with an illuminance of 250 to 400 lux, and ensure that it’s well-lit from all sides.
Avoid warm or any other colored light sources.
The programming steps to scan and define a reference object that ARKit can use for detection are simple.
Build and run this app on your iOS device to walk through a series of steps for getting high-quality scan data, resulting in reference object files that you can use for detection in your own apps.
To ensure timely compression for the real-time streaming use case of the app, the video processor enables the compression session’s option.
An app may require different virtual content, such as an arrow that points to a precise spot, or virtual text that explains the importance of a location.
Use a tracked accessory with Apple Vision Pro to create a virtual sculpture.
You can use ARKit to locate and track accessories, converting their real-world motion into app-accessible data.
Some experiences may require accessories, but most let people perform tasks with both controllers and hands.
Additionally, a entry with a type is required for the framework to return controllers when requested.
If at least one controller is connected and located within the volume bounds, the app state updates accordingly.
To complete the effect of augmenting an image in the user’s environment, you use ARKit’s image tracking feature.
As shown below, you can use Vision in real-time to check the camera feed for rectangles.
When you make Vision requests in real-time with an ARKit–based app, you should do so serially.
By waiting for one request to finish before invoking another, you ensure that the AR experience remains smooth and free of interruptions.
In the function, you use the flag to ensure you’re only checking for one rectangle at a time: The sample sets the flag to when a Vision request completes or fails.
ARKit requires that reference images contain sufficient detail to be recognizable; for example, a plain white image cannot be tracked.
To prevent ARKit from failing to track a reference image, you validate it first before attempting to use it.
The face mesh provided by ARKit, showing automatic estimation of the real-world directional lighting environment, as well as a texture you can use to map 2D imagery onto the face.
A simple robot character whose facial expression animates to match that of the user, showing how to use ARKit’s animation blend shape values to create experiences like the system Animoji app.
Use the tab bar to switch between these modes.
Face tracking is available on all devices with Apple Neural Engine in iOS 14 & iPad OS 14, and requires a device with TrueDepth camera on iOS 13 & iPadOS 13 and below.
Like other uses of ARKit, face tracking requires configuring and running a session (an object) and rendering the camera image together with virtual content in a view.
This sample uses to display 3D content with SceneKit, but you can also use SpriteKit or build your own renderer using Metal (see and .
Face tracking differs from other uses of ARKit in the class you use to configure the session.
Your AR experience can use this mesh to place or draw content that appears to attach to the face.
You can use the image included with this sample code project as a starting point to design your own face textures.
Another use of the face mesh that ARKit provides is to create occlusion geometry in your scene.
ARKit requires iOS 11 and a device with an A9 (or later) processor.
Building the sample code requires Xcode 9 or later.
The class is a SceneKit view that includes an object that manages the motion tracking and image processing required to create an augmented reality (AR) experience.
However, to run a session you must provide a session configuration.
If your app requires ARKit for its core functionality, use the key in the section of your app’s file to make your app available only on devices that support ARKit.
If AR is a secondary feature of your app, use the property to determine whether to offer AR-based features.
After you’ve set up your AR session, you can use SceneKit to place virtual content in the view.
The sample app demonstrates geotracking coaching, which requires iOS 15.
Geotracking requires a device with A12 Bionic chip or later, and cellular (GPS) capability.
To place location anchors with precision, geotracking requires a better understanding of the user’s geographic location than is possible with GPS alone.
ARKit requires a network connection to download localization imagery.
At any point, the session can require action from the user to progress to the next state.
A geotracking session maps geographic coordinates to ARKit’s world-tracking local space, which requires basic world-tracking support.
For example, if the user travels too quickly, the device’s camera feed may not contain sufficient features that ARKit requires to model the environment.
To ensure the best possible user experience, an app must monitor and react to the geotracking .
The sample specifies the allowable-target, and alignment option, as required to retrieve a point on a meshed, real-world object.
When the property of is , RealityKit disables classification by default because it isn’t required for occlusion and physics.
To make the text always appear the same size on screen, the sample applies a scale based on text’s distance from the camera.
This sample can run on the following: An Apple Vision Pro with visionOS 2 or later Use room tracking in visionOS to provide custom interactions with physical spaces.
